Please describe your research career to date (1000 words)
Previous Experience:
I am an early-career researcher with a strong interest in improving evidence synthesis in women’s
health. I have a BSc in Physiology (McGill University), an MSc in Population Health (UCL), and
experience working on a variety of health research projects.
As an undergraduate, I spent two summers as a research assistant with Dr Rosemary Martino,
University of Toronto. Initially, I evaluated abstracts for inclusion in a systematic review assessing
the benefits of dysphagia screening in stroke patients. This introduced me to systematic reviews
and gave me insight into their importance in healthcare research. I subsequently helped develop an
online mixed-methods survey of speech-language pathologist practices in paediatric dysphagia;
developing skills in SPSS by tabulating survey response frequencies, means

secrc__001_g.text:
and standard
deviations (REF).
During undergraduate courses in reproductive physiology, I was struck by the paucity of evidence
in areas of women’s health. I sought work on a cross-sectional study investigating the relationship
between hypertensive disorders of pregnancy and postpartum depression. Under Dr Natalie Dayan
(Research Institute, McGill University Health Centre), I gained first-hand experience extracting
clinical data from medical records into RedCap for analysis. Importantly, this project introduced
me to women’s health research and furthered my interests in this area. I have remained involved in
this study as a manuscript co-author.
Prior to my Masters, I worked with Dr Samira Mubareka (Sunnybrook Research Institute) as a part of
the Royal Society of Canada’s One Health Working Group. I participated in group meetings and
helped to develop and write a policy brief about a One Health approach to zoonoses (REF). This
advanced my writing skills, and I gained experience collaborating with a large multi-disciplinary
team, including a variety of experts, as well as representatives from government and Canada’s
Indigenous communities. This work taught me the importance of and h

secrc__001_h.text:
ow to synthesise existing
literature to inform policy.
My MSc in Population Health introduced me to study designs used in medical research, including
clinical trials and systematic reviews, and statistical approaches like regression analyses. I also
gained valuable experience designing and conducting analyses in my dissertation where I
investigated sex differences in the relationship between blood pressure and depression over the
life course, using data from the 1946 British Birth Cohort. I have continued to collaborate on this
project to produce a manuscript for publication and have presented preliminary results at a UCL
departmental meeting. Completion of this degree, and particularly the dissertation, further
motivated me to pursue a career in women’s health.
Following my master's degree, I joined the London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine Clinical
Trials Unit as Assistant Trial Manager for the international I’M WOMAN trial. Working on this trial

secrc__001_i.text:
further solidified my interest in women’s health research and showed me the huge impact research
can have on the lives of women. It gave me greater insight and experience in clinical trial design and
implementation, writing ethics applications, and recruiting trial sites. In addition, I learned
important transferrable skills through managing multiple tasks simultaneously and effectively
resolving unexpected circumstances that can arise during a study.
Current Employment:
In November 2023, I started my current position (Research Assistant, Meta-Analysis Group, MRC
Clinical Trials Unit at UCL), funded by a National Institute of Health Research Health Technology
Assessment Award (NIHR153187), to synthesise results from clinical trials which have assessed
treatments for heavy menstrual bleeding. This role has combined my research interests in
women’s health and evidence synthesis.
My duties have included developing protocols, conducting literature searches, identifying eligible
clinical trials, developing data extraction forms, and completing data extraction across 120 eligible
trials. Importantly, I helped to host a workshop involving women with lived experience of heavy
menstrual ble

secrc__001_j.text:
eding to inform the design of a mixed-methods survey to understand the impact of
heavy periods on quality of life and women’s experiences seeking treatment. I subsequently
supervised MSc students to seek ethical approval, develop and analyse this survey, a manuscript
for which is in preparation. I have also supervised students to undertake risk of bias assessments
and have overseen a visiting student to assess trustworthiness of the trials. I have worked with the
lead statistician to develop an analysis plan and establish meta-analyses of the primary outcomes.
Results from these meta-analyses are anticipated in May 2025. Complexities that have arisen in
this process have been a key motivation for developing my Fellowship proposal.
In early 2024, I was central in organising a Menstrual Health Research Day, to inform women,
patient advocates and women’s health charity representatives (e.g. Wellbeing of Women,
Menstrual Health Project) about our research. During the event, I presented our research plans
and sought input from attendees through a live survey and discussion regarding which outcomes
should be prioritised.
I was also invited to co-author a WHO-commissioned Cochrane review

secrc__001_k.text:
comparing intravenous
versus oral iron in treating iron-deficiency anaemia in pregnancy (REF). As first author, I identified
and evaluated relevant clinical trials, collected and meta-analysed data, completed risk of bias,
trustworthiness and GRADE assessments for each outcome included in the review, as well as
contributed to writing the manuscript. From this, I gained extensive experience in every stage of
conducting systematic reviews and meta-analyses and expanded my network among other
researchers.
Impact of the Fellowship
This fellowship will be an important step towards my long-term career aim to become a leading
methodologist and researcher in women’s health.
Through my proposed research plan, formal trainings, and with support from my supervisors and
collaborators, I will further my understanding of more complex research methods in evidence
synthesis, including statistical and qualitative methodologies. The fellowship will also enable me to

secrc__001_l.text:
expand my research networks within and outside academia, through proposed research visits,
collaborations and attending conferences. It will also enable me to communicate results of my
research, through publications and conference presentations, to establish a track record in this
area. Importantly, I will develop the skills needed for me to become an independent researcher and
progress as a leader in evidence synthesis for women’s health.

Plain English Summary of Research (600 words)
Background Information
One in four women will have heavy periods, which means they lose a lot of blood during their period
or have a period that lasts a long time. They might also feel tired, have pain and need to miss days
of school or work. These things can all make women’s lives harder.
Researchers have tested treatments for women with heavy periods in clinical trials. In these
clinical trials, researchers measure how much blood a woman loses on her period before she has
treatment and after she has treatment to see if there is a difference in the amount of blood she
loses. Researchers can measure blood loss in a laboratory, or women can record how their used
pads or tampons l

secrc__001_m.text:
ook using picture charts, or answer questionnaires.
Researchers bring together information from all these trials in research called a meta-analysis,
which is a good way to understand which treatments work best. But because blood loss has been
measured in different ways in each clinical trial, it is hard to do a meta-analysis. That means it has
been hard to find out if some treatments work better than others.
Aim and Objectives
In the first part of my research, I will figure out how to best bring together the results from clinical
trials to get better evidence about which treatments reduce bleeding the most. Then I will do
research to understand what doctors, researchers and women think about how blood loss is
measured in clinical trials. This will help researchers do better clinical trials in the future.
Research Methods
I will use a mixture of different research methods during this project. For the first part of my project,
I will carry out a thorough review of all the clinical trials to find all the ways blood loss has been
measured. I will then try to use different statistical methods to find better ways to combine results
from clinical trials that have tested treatments for hea

secrc__001_n.text:
vy periods.
For the second part I will speak to women, and groups of doctors and researchers to find out their
opinions on ways of measuring blood loss in clinical trials.
Patient and Public Involvement (PPI)

secrc__001_o.text:
I have spoken to four women who have lived with heavy periods about my research plans. They
agree it is important to do this research so that women with heavy periods get the right treatment.
They noted that it would be important to speak to women of different ages, races, or cultural
backgrounds to understand different opinions on being in clinical trials that measure blood loss.
Therefore, I have planned how to include a diverse group of women in my research interviews.
I plan to set up a PPI group of six women with experience of heavy periods. I will meet with this
group twice each year to share my progress, get their feedback on what I have done, and advice for
my next steps. They will help me to recruit women for my research interviews and give me advice on
what questions I should ask. They will also help me to present my findings so that members of the
public can understand them and find them helpful.
Sharing Results
I will present my research at conferences and write papers for medical journals. I will also
communicate my results more widely, including using social media, blogs, or podcasts so that
people affected by heavy periods hear them. I will ask advice on the best way

secrc__001_p.text:
s to do this from my
PPI group.
Scientific Abstract (500 words)
Background
Heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) affects one in four women of reproductive age. HMB is a life
altering symptom which impacts all aspects of women’s quality of life. Alongside the physical
symptoms, women with HMB also often experience isolation from work and social activities due to
fears of bleeding through clothing.
There are many causes of HMB, however, many women also suffer from HMB with no identified
pathology. There are numerous treatments available to women with HMB, but there is limited high
quality evidence comparing treatment options. Although many clinical trials have compared
different treatments, there is large variability in how outcomes from these trials are reported. For
example, some HMB trials measure menstrual blood loss (MBL) using the alkaline-hematin method
or variations of pictorial blood assessment charts, while other trials report subjective measures of
MBL. This poses challenges for evidence synthesis, as oftentimes many trials are excluded from
meta-analyses.
A Core Outcome Set (COS) was published for HMB trials which identified nine outcomes to be
collected in future HMB trials. On

secrc__001_q.text:
e of the outcomes identified in the COS was menstrual blood loss
(MBL). However, the COS did not specify which measurement tools should be used to measure
MBL, or which timepoints should be reported. Therefore, there is still a significant need for further
work to be done to standardise and improve meta-analyses of the MBL outcome in HMB trials.

secrc__001_r.text:
Research Question
How can we improve meta-analyses of MBL outcomes in clinical trials comparing treatment
options for HMB?
Objectives
1. To critically assess the measurement tools used to assess MBL in clinical trials.
2. To explore statistical approaches to improve meta-analysis for the different MBL outcomes
reported.
3. To determine what is acceptable and prioritised to women and trialists when measuring
MBL in HMB trials.
Methods and Timelines for Delivery
Work Package 1 (Months 1-8) - Conducting a systematic review of the literature to identify
different measurement tools used to assess MBL in clinical trials and critically evaluating these
tools.
Work Package 2 (Months 9-18) - Exploring statistical approaches for meta-analyses and applying
these so that more clinical trial data is included in meta-analyses of the MBL outcome.
Work Package 3 (Months 19-30) - Conducting interviews and focus groups with women and trial
teams to determine which aspects of MBL measures are acceptable and prioritised by different
stakeholders.
Anticipated Impact and Dissemination
The proposed research will inform the choice of MBL measurement tools in future trials and will
provide better estimates

secrc__001_s.text:
of the effect of treatments on MBL using data from past clinical trials. The
findings of each work package will be disseminated in scientific journals and presented at relevant
conferences. The PPI committee will be crucial in advising how to communicate findings in a way
that is easily understandable to the public. These could include social media posts, blogs and
podcasts to disseminate findings to the wider public.

secrc__001_t.text:
Detailed Research Plan (5000 words)
What is the problem being addressed?
Despite many clinical trials, systematic reviews and a recently developed core outcome set (COS),
evidence regarding which treatments are most effective for women (and those born female) who
experience heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) remains limited.
This is because trials to date have reported results inconsistently, or evaluated treatments using
different outcome measures, which poses challenges for evidence synthesis. The COS provided a
list of nine outcomes that it recommended should be implemented in future HMB trials. However,
it did not specify how these key outcomes like menstrual blood loss (MBL) should be measured,
meaning that complexity in the synthesis of trial results persists. Therefore, further work is vital to
standardise outcome measurement and prevent inconsistencies in HMB trials going forward.
My proposed research aims to improve meta-analyses of MBL outcomes from HMB trials to better
inform treatment guidelines and help improve the lives women suffering with HMB. I will explore
how statistical methods might be used to better combine MBL data from existing HMB trials in
meta-analyses. I wil

secrc__001_u.text:
l also determine the priorities of women and trialists when measuring MBL to
inform consistent MBL measurement in future HMB trials.
Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and/or
wellbeing of the public and/or to patients/service users, carers, and health
and care services?
An estimated one in four women of reproductive age are affected by heavy menstrual bleeding
(HMB)(REF). Women with HMB suffer lower quality of life due to both physical symptoms as well as
restrictions on their social and work lives. Women with menstrual health conditions make up a key
proportion of the 755,000 women waiting for gynaecology services in the UK, with many women
waiting over a year for treatment (REF). These delays in diagnosis and treatment leave women
suffering worsening symptoms and poor quality of life for extended periods of time. This can also
lead to more complex treatments being needed when they are eventually seen by gynaecology
services. Therefore, it is crucial that HMB research is prioritised now.
While there are many medical and surgical treatment options available to treat those who
experience HMB, high-quality evidence synthesis to guide policy and practice i

secrc__001_v.text:
s limited. The
women we have spoken to have stressed the need for better quality evidence as currently many
women try various treatments before finding one which is effective. The evidence of treatment
effectiveness has been hindered in a large part by inconsistency of outcome measurements used
across relevant clinical trials. For example, menstrual blood loss (MBL) and quality of life (QoL)
have been evaluated using a range of instruments, and at a variety of time-points, which
complicates meta-analysis and reduces reliability of findings.

secrc__001_w.text:
Therefore, this research is vitally important to people affected by HMB. The proposed research will
provide more reliable evidence about which treatments are most effective in reducing MBL and
reduce research waste from trials that currently are not contributing to evidence syntheses. It will
also explore whether reduced MBL leads to improvement in other outcomes that are important to
women, including QoL. This is based on feedback from PPI groups that reduced MBL is only
important if it also results in improved QoL. Lastly, this project will provide foundations for the
development of a measure or tool that can be used to reliably measure MBL in clinical trials, taking
the needs of women (including women from different age-groups, regions and ethnicities), trialists,
and other stakeholders (e.g. guideline developers, regulatory authorities) into account. The current
measures of MBL used in clinical trials can be burdensome, and it is unclear whether these
measures are acceptable to all women. Therefore, this research might greatly improve women’s
participation in future trials, and the applicability of future trials and evidence syntheses.
Ultimately, this will facilitate efficient

secrc__001_x.text:
and effective treatment strategies for women suffering from
HMB and greatly improve their quality of life.
Review of the existing evidence – how does the existing literature support this
proposal?
What is known about heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) and how does it impact on those
affected?
Approximately one in four women of reproductive age experience HMB, which accounts for
approximately 20% of gynaecology referrals in the NHS (REFs). It is also likely the prevalence of
HMB has been underestimated due to normalisation of symptoms and social stigma surrounding
menstruation. There are many possible causes of HMB which can be classified using the FIGO
classification system 2 (PALM-COEIN) (REF). The FIGO classification system categorises causes
into either structural causes (Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy and hyperplasia) or
non-structural causes (Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet
classified) (REF). However, the mechanisms behind which many of these causes lead to HMB is
still not understood.
HMB is a symptom with life altering implications for women who suffer from it. For example, HMB is
associated with poorer self-rated physical health

secrc__001_y.text:
with women experiencing symptoms of shortness
of breath, weakness, and painful periods (Sinharoy 2023, Karlsson 2013). HMB is also a leading
cause of iron deficiency and iron deficiency anaemia in women ( Munro 2023). Alongside physical
symptoms, women often need to plan activities around their bleeding due to the frequent need to
change menstrual products and fear of embarrassment associated with bleeding through
menstrual products (Karlsson 2013). The variety of limitations that women experience because of
HMB have significant negative impacts on their social life and emotional wellbeing. There are also
significant economic costs of HMB, with estimations that absenteeism or presenteeism at work
due to HMB costs the UK about £11 billion per year (REF).

secrc__001_z.text:
In research settings and previous clinical guidelines, HMB has been defined as menstrual blood
loss (MBL) >80ml or a duration of >7days. However, more recently, the National Institute for Health
and Care Excellence (NICE) has changed guidelines to define HMB as excessive menstrual blood
loss which interferes with a woman’s physical, social, emotional and/or material quality of life
(REF). Previous studies suggest that many women only seek treatment for HMB when they fail to
manage symptoms themselves due to the feeling that their symptoms will be dismissed by medical
professionals as ‘trivial’ (Santer 2008). There are also disparities in prevalence of HMB in different
populations. For example, many women begin to experience HMB in perimenopause (REF), and
certain conditions which cause HMB, such as uterine fibroids, are more common in Black women
(REF).
What are the limitations regarding evidence for HMB treatments?
There are many different treatment options available to women suffering from HMB. These include
medical and hormonal treatments, such as contraceptives or tranexamic acid, as well as
procedures or surgeries, such as endometrial ablation or hysterectomy. Since many treat

secrc__001_{.text:
ments
result in infertility or are contraceptive in nature, women who wish for future children or who are
actively trying to conceive may have limited treatment options. Therefore, there is a need for
treatments options to be assessed using a life course approach to reflect changes in women’s
treatment preferences throughout their lifetime.
Current treatment guidance in the UK for those with no identified pathology recommends using a
levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) as first-line treatment based (REF).
However, the evidence underpinning treatment recommendations is of low certainty for outcomes
such as patient satisfaction and quality of life. High-quality evidence comparing treatments in
women of different demographic groups or with different causes of HMB is needed. This current
lack of evidence for treatments in different populations often leads women to trying many
treatment options before finding one which is effective. This leads to long time suffering for women
with HMB, with many eventually opting for drastic treatments such as hysterectomies (REF).
One major reason for the of lack high-quality evidence is that across the existing clinical trials
which

secrc__001_|.text:
have evaluated treatments for HMB, there is significant variation in which outcomes have
been reported and how they have been measured (Cooper 2022). As a result of this, a Core
Outcome Set (COS) was developed which identified nine outcomes which should be reported in
future HMB trials (Cooper 2023). While the COS provides a standardised list of outcomes including
menstrual blood loss (MBL), flooding, menstrual cycle metrics, dysmenorrhea, condition-specific
and generic quality of life, adverse events, satisfaction, treatment failure and haemoglobin for
trials, it does not indicate how each of these outcomes should be measured.
MBL is the most reported outcome in HMB trials, however, MBL has been assessed and reported in
many ways (Cooper 2022, Herman 2016). These include quantitative measures of MBL such as the
alkaline-hematin method which is a lab-based method of measuring the blood volume in women’s
menstrual products (REF). Many trials also use versions of pictorial blood loss assessment charts
which have been developed (REFs). These pictorial charts are used by women to count the number

secrc__001_}.text:
of menstrual products they use and how soiled these products are. However, many of these have
not been validated with modern menstrual products (Magnay 2020). In addition to the quantitative
measures used in trials, there are also many subjective measures of MBL such as having women
rate whether their bleeding is better or worse than before treatment.
Each method has its own associated limitations, both in terms of acceptability to women, and in
consistency or accuracy of the measurement. The different MBL measurement tools have been
previously reviewed, (Magnay 2018), however, this review did not critically assess each tool using
the strict methodology that exists for reviewing measurement tools (COSMIN), and there have been
several studies published which aim to develop or validate MBL measures since the previous
review from 2018.
The array of measurement tools is further compounded by trials making assessments of blood loss
outcomes at a variety of timepoints and using different statistical summaries to report their
findings.
Therefore, there is an urgent need for work in this area to develop guidance on how outcomes
should be measured. Otherwise, the problem of inconsistent out

secrc__001_~.text:
comes will get worse as more
clinical trials are developed without clarity on how to measure outcomes.
Challenges for Evidence Synthesis
The variations in outcome measurements reported in HMB trials, particularly MBL outcomes, pose
significant challenges for systematic reviews and meta-analyses. To date, meta-analyses
comparing the treatments of HMB have been forced to exclude many trials because of differences
in measurement tools used or timepoints reported (Bofill Rodriguez 2022). This exclusion of trials
from meta-analyses results not only wasting of research but also reduces the ability to make
conclusions about the effectiveness of different treatment options. This causes delays in the
implementation of new effective treatments and may contribute to or exacerbate inequities in
treatment for women with HMB.
Various statistical methods exist which may help incorporate more trial data into meta-analyses.
These include investigations into the correlations between different measures or timepoints and
incorporating meta-analytic priors based on these correlations (REF). It is also possible to
incorporate trials which have reported different outcomes using multivariate meta-analysis

secrc__001_.text:
methods (Hattle 2022, Riley 2017). However, no meta-analysis has investigated the use of these
more complex meta-analysis approaches in the context of HMB trials.
What is the research question/aims and objectives?
Research Question: How to improve meta-analyses of menstrual blood loss outcomes from heavy
menstrual bleeding clinical trials to facilitate evidence-based treatment?
Objectives:

secrc__001_.text:
1) To evaluate the different outcome measures used to compare menstrual blood loss in
clinical trials.
2) To apply different statistical approaches to improve meta-analyses of menstrual blood loss
measures.
3) To determine which outcome measurements of menstrual blood loss would be acceptable
and prioritised by different stakeholders for future clinical trials.
Project Plan
This research project will consist of four work packages each associated with one of the aims listed
above. The work packages include complementary quantitative and qualitative research
components, which will be used to address the overall research question.
Work Package for Aim 1: Identifying and evaluating measurement tools used for assessing
menstrual blood loss in clinical trials.
Many different measures of menstrual blood loss (MBL) have been used in clinical trials. There is a
need to understand how these different measures compare and to critically appraise these
measures for use in heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) trials.
Reviewing Measures of MBL in Clinical Trials
I will systematically review the literature for all HMB randomised controlled trials which have
collected MBL as an outcome. As part of my tea

secrc__001_.text:
m’s current work conducting a series of systematic
reviews of treatments for HMB, we have identified 114 published trials which have collected MBL
as an outcome (NIHR grant number), however we have not appraised the different measures of
MBL used. As part of my fellowship, I will update the previous searches in CENTRAL, EMBASE,
MEDLINE and trial registries to identify any new trials which have collected MBL as an outcome.
In the current series of reviews, we have only extracted MBL data from trials which reported
continuous measures based on the alkaline-hematin method or pictorial charts (70 trials). For my
fellowship, I will collect information on the MBL measures reported across all HMB trials identified.
I will also collect information on trial dates, the recruitment rates, losses to follow up, and the
characteristics of women included. I will then use this data to identify whether MBL measures used
in trials have changed over time, whether they differ by population included, and whether
participant recruitment or dropout rates are correlated with the MBL measure used.
Critically Appraising Available Measures of MBL
I will conduct an update to previous systematic review (Magnay

secrc__001_.text:
2018) to identify any new studies
which have developed or validated new or existing MBL measures. I will also search for any
qualitative studies which have assessed any measures of MBL. Updated searches will be
conducted in CENTRAL, EMBASE, MEDLINE and clinical trial registries (Clinicaltrials.gov, WHO
ICTRP).

secrc__001_.text:
For included studies which have developed or validated measures of MBL, I will collect data on the
characteristics of the MBL measure used, the study samples, the results of the MBL measure and
information on the feasibility and interpretability of each MBL measure. I will use the COSMIN Risk
of Bias checklist for patient-reported outcome measures (REF) to assess the methodological
quality of each study. I will also use COSMIN criteria for good measurement properties to rate
whether each MBL measure is sufficient, insufficient or indeterminate (REF).
PPI Committee
I will meet with my PPI committee prior to starting data collection to seek advice on what
information is important to collect from each study to properly review the measures of MBL.
Towards the end of this work package, I will meet with my PPI committee to present my results and
decide the best way to disseminate my findings.
Findings
The findings from this literature review will be reported to help inform recommendations for choice
of MBL measures for future trials. These findings will also provide a comprehensive list of MBL
measures which will be further investigated in work packages 2 and 3.
Work Package for Aim 2: D

secrc__001_.text:
eveloping and applying statistical approaches to improve how MBL
outcome measures are combined in meta-analyses.
Variations in how and when MBL is measured, and how it is reported, present complex challenges
for evidence synthesis. Subsets of trials with consistent handling of MBL data may be analysed
whilst other evidence is excluded, with clear implications for risk-of-bias and strength of evidence.
Attempts to synthesise more comprehensively may result in confused or biased interpretations
due to data variations not being handled appropriately. This work package will explore different
statistical approaches that may help to meta-analyse data more appropriately and completely
from trials where a measure of MBL is reported.
MBL Reported at Different Timepoints
Post-treatment measurement of MBL may take place at various lengths of time after
randomisation, typically between 3 and 12 months. To address this, I will investigate the strength of
correlation between MBL measures collected at different timepoints (for example, 3 and 6
months), and thereby assess whether it is more appropriate to analyse such data separately or
together. For example, do MBL measures collected at 3 months

secrc__001_.text:
differ significantly from those
collected at 6 months, or can they be assumed to be equivalent for the purpose of meta-analysis?
MBL Measured with Different Tools
As mentioned in previous sections, there are a variety of different measurement tools which can be
used to measure MBL, such as the alkaline-hematin method or pictorial blood assessment charts.
Based on the different tools identified in work package 1, I will investigate within-study correlations
between tools from validation studies or from HMB trials which reported using more than one

secrc__001_.text:
measurement of MBL. I will then explore statistical approaches, such as the use of Bayesian priors,
to determine which MBL measures could be combined in meta-analyses.
Differences in MBL Effect Measures and Analysis Methods
Another issue which makes meta-analysis of the MBL outcome challenging is the variety of effect
measures used in trial reports. For example, MBL may be analysed as a continuous measure, or
may be dichotomised into “success” or “failure”. Continuous measurement may be summarised
using means, medians or percentage change from baseline. For binary (dichotomised) data,
analysis may be via contingency tables or logistic regression. Data may be reported as raw
differences between arms or might be adjusted for baseline differences or other prognostic factors.
I will investigate existing statistical methods which have been developed to estimate continuous
measures from binary measures, or which are used to convert medians to means. I will determine
whether these existing statistical approaches can be validated in the context of the MBL outcome.
The Relationship Between MBL and Quality of Life (QoL)
While MBL is an important objective outcome in trials comparing treatmen

secrc__001_.text:
ts of HMB, we have
received feedback from women with lived experience of HMB stressing that reduction in MBL is
only meaningful if this relates to improved QoL. As part of this work package, I will investigate the
correlations between MBL and QoL in trials which have reported both outcomes. I will then explore
whether statistical methods such as multivariate meta-analysis might be used to synthesise both
outcomes simultaneously to provide results which are more meaningful to women.
PPI Committee
As this work package is using more complex statistical methods, my PPI committee will be very
helpful in advising whether combining this data into meta-analyses is still useful to women. I will
also meet with my PPI committee to seek advice on what language is appropriate and easily
understandable, and how I might visually present my results to the public.
Findings
The findings from this work package will provide insights on the more complex meta-analysis
methods which can be used to assess treatment effects on MBL. This will allow more precise
estimates of the effects of treatments on MBL than previous meta-analyses by including data from
more trials.
Work Package for Aim 3: Determining wh

secrc__001_.text:
ich measures of menstrual blood loss are acceptable
and prioritised by women and trial investigators.
It is necessary to understand what is most important and acceptable to all relevant stakeholders to
determine which MBL measure should be used in clinical trials.
In this work package, I will conduct focus groups and interviews with women with lived experience
of HMB and HMB clinical trial investigators and staff to understand which measures are acceptable
to different stakeholders. I will use a mixture of online and in person discussions to better develop

secrc__001_.text:
my skills in conducting interviews and focus groups. The topic guides for the interviews and focus
groups will be developed based on findings from my formal evaluations of different MBL measures
in work package 1.
Interviews: Women with lived experience of HMB
Due to the potentially personal nature of discussing HMB and MBL, I have chosen to conduct
interviews with women with lived experience of HMB instead of focus groups. I will aim to recruit
15-20 women for interviews using purposive sampling. I will examine what women find acceptable
when needing to measure MBL and whether the MBL measure used in clinical trials impacts
whether women choose to participate in trials. These interviews will also investigate whether the
barriers to participating in HMB trials differ among women from different demographic groups.
To recruit women to interview, I plan to utilise the extensive PPI networks of Wellbeing of Women
through my clinical collaborator, Prof Hilary Critchley and through my collaborators at Menstrual
Health Project, Anna Cooper and Gabriella Pearson. I will also create adverts for recruitment which
will be posted on the MRC CTU’s social media and the NIHR People in Research we

secrc__001_.text:
bsite. These
adverts will contain a link to a form which will collect basic data on the demographics of people
interested to participate. I will then use purposive sampling to ensure that I interview women from a
wide range of demographics. The demographic characteristics of the women interviewed will be
included in the publication of the findings from this work package.
Focus Groups: HMB trial investigators and staff
To understand how and why trial investigators have chosen to use a specific MBL measure in their
HMB trials, I will conduct focus groups with trial teams. I will aim to conduct four focus groups with
trial teams each with about five participants. In the focus groups, I will examine whether trial teams
considered different measures of MBL, and which factors led to their decision to use the MBL
measure they chose. These discussions will also cover whether trialists experienced feasibility
issues or whether they felt participant dropouts were dependent on the MBL measure collected.
To recruit trial staff, I will use the networks I have gained through working at the MRC CTU at UCL on
a series of systematic reviews of treatments for HMB. While working on these reviews, I h

secrc__001_.text:
ave
established communications with investigators from some of the HMB trials.
Data Analysis
These focus group and interview discussions will be audio recorded and transcribed. The data from
these focus groups will be analysed in NVivo using a mixture of deductive and inductive reflexive
thematic analysis.
Consensus Workshop: Representatives from different stakeholder groups
If the interview and focus group findings indicate that there are differences in what MBL measures
are prioritised by women versus trial staff, I will hold a consensus workshop where members from
each group will discuss together. The aim of the consensus workshop would be for each group to

secrc__001_.text:
express their thoughts to each other and determine whether consensus about which MBL
measures are most important can be reached. This will be used to help interpret the data from the
interview and focus group analyses.
PPI Committee
For this work package, I will seek advice from my PPI committee to determine which demographic
groups are particularly important to reach in interviews to inform sampling. I will also do a data
clinic with my PPI committee where I will present some of the codes and initial themes I have found
to get their thoughts on my interpretation of the data.
Findings
The findings from this work package will be used to determine what needs to be incorporated into a
tool to measure MBL. This may mean that an existing measure of MBL could be recommended for
use in future HMB trials or may show there is a need for development of a new tool incorporating
these findings.
Dissemination, outputs and anticipated impact
Dissemination and Planned Outputs
Peer-Reviewed Publications and Conference Presentations
The findings from each work package will be submitted as articles to peer-reviewed journals and I
will also attend conferences throughout my fellowship to present my fi

ndings.
Work Package 1: I will publish the critical assessments of MBL measurement tools in a relevant
women’s health journal to inform their use in future HMB trials. I will also aim to present these
findings at a relevant conference such as International Clinical Trials Methodology Conference at
the end of year 1 of my fellowship.
Work Package 2: I will publish an article based on my findings from applying complex statistical
approaches to meta-analyse the MBL outcome in a relevant methodology journal to help inform
their use in other meta-analyses. I will also present these findings at a relevant evidence synthesis
conference, such as the Cochrane Colloquium at the end of year 2.
Work Package 3: I plan to present the findings from my focus group and interview discussions at a
relevant women’s health conference in year 3. I will also submit these findings for publication in a
relevant women’s health journal.

I plan to share the findings of my research project with help my collaborators, Prof Hilary Critchley
at Wellbeing of Women and Anna Cooper and Gabriella Pearson at the Menstrual Health Project.
Both Wellbeing of Women and the Menstrual He

networks of women with lived experience of HMB. I will also utilise the social media accounts from
the MRC CTU at UCL to make posts or podcasts to disseminate my findings.
My PPI committee will be crucial in developing plans to disseminate my findings to women with
HMB and other members of the public. I will be guided by my PPI committee on how to best
disseminate my findings so they are easily understandable and accessible to a wide audience.
Anticipated Impact of Research Findings
Informing Future Research
The findings of my research will have significant impact on trialists conducting future trials in
women with HMB. I will present the findings of my work to the COS working group with the help of
my secondary supervisor, Dr Natalie Cooper. Based on my findings, I will participate in discussions
of whether an appropriate measure of MBL already exists which can be recommended for future
trials. If there is no appropriate measure based on my findings, we will discuss the best ways to
develop an MBL measure as part of a future body of work.
My findings from applying different statistical approaches to meta-analyse MBL outcome measures
could also inform researchers of how to deal wit

h variations in outcome reporting in future meta-
analyses of HMB trials, or in other clinical areas.
Informing Guideline Developers and Regulatory Processes to Improve Health
As part of my training plan, I will do a work placement with the MHRA which will be organised with
the help of my collaborator Dr Jahnavi Daru. During this work placement, I will have the opportunity
to present my findings and explore how my findings might facilitate more efficient decision making
within the agency around licensing of treatments.
I will also use my team’s current collaborations with NICE to explore how my research findings may
be incorporated into future NICE guidelines for treatment of HMB.
The Impact on Women and the NHS
Apart from the significant impacts of my findings on future research in HMB, my work to better
estimate the effects of treatments on MBL and QoL will inform guidelines and medical practice. By
making use of all the available trials and reducing research waste, my findings will help to
efficiently provide women with effective treatment options for HMB. This will lead to significantly
improved quality of life for women with HMB and will reduce the burden of HMB within the NHS

.

This fellowship will be an important step towards my long-term career aim to become a leading
methodologist and researcher in women’s health.
Through my proposed research plan, formal trainings, and with support from my supervisors and
collaborators, I will further my understanding of more complex research methods in evidence
synthesis, including statistical and qualitative methodologies. The fellowship will also enable me to

expand my research networks within and outside academia, through proposed research visits,
collaborations and attending conferences. It will also enable me to communicate results of my
research, through publications and conference presentations, to establish a track record in this
area. Importantly, I will develop the skills needed for me to become an independent researcher and
progress as a leader in evidence synthesis for women’s health.

Plain English Summary of Research (600 words)
Background Information
One in four women will have heavy periods, which means they lose a lot of blood during their period
or have a period that lasts a long time. They might also feel tired, have pain and need to miss days
of school or work. These things can all make women’s lives harder.
Researchers have tested treatments for women with heavy periods in clinical trials. In these
clinical trials, researchers measure how much blood a woman loses on her period before she has
treatment and after she has treatment to see if there is a difference in the amount of blood she
loses. Researchers can measure blood loss in a laboratory, or women can record how their used
pads or tampons l

.

(secondary supervisor), Mr David Fisher (secondary supervisor) and Dr Natalie Cooper (secondary

milestones, helping her to manage timelines, and to upgrade and complete on time.
Importantly, Claire will support Lily to make decisions regarding the direction of her
research and manage potentially conflicting priorities. She will lead the supervisory panel.
Claire and Lily will meet weekly initially, moving to fortnightly as Lily progresses through
her PhD. Claire will help Lily to set up a Thesis Committee, whose role is to provide degree-
spanning, independent support and advice about academic and training progress.
Claire has supervised 2 PhD and 2 MSc students. She was appointed as Departmental
Graduate Tutor (Research) for the UCL Institute of Clinical Trials and Methodology in 2018,
overseeing the Institute’s post-graduate research (PGR) programme and contributing to
Faculty PGR oversight. She is an active member of the MRC-NIHR Trials and Methodology
Research Partnership Doctoral Training Partnership (TMRP DTP) Leadership Team,
supporting doctoral students across the breadth of clinical trials methodology.

contributed to writing the manuscript. From this, I gained extensive experience in every stage of
conducting systematic reviews and meta-analyses and expanded my network among other
researchers.
Impact of the Fellowship
This fellowship will be an important step towards my long-term career aim to become a leading
methodologist and researcher in women’s health.
Through my proposed research plan, formal trainings, and with support from my supervisors and
collaborators, I will further my understanding of more complex research methods in evidence
synthesis, including statistical and qualitative methodologies. The fellowship will also enable me to

expand my research networks within and outside academia, through proposed research visits,
collaborations and attending conferences. It will also enable me to communicate results of my
research, through publications and conference presentations, to establish a track record in this
area. Importantly, I will develop the skills needed for me to become an independent researcher and
progress as a leader in evidence synthesis for women’s health.

Plain English Summary of Research (600 words)
Background Information
One in four women will have heavy periods, which means they lose a lot of blood during their period
or have a period that lasts a long time. They might also feel tired, have pain and need to miss days
of school or work. These things can all make women’s lives harder.
Researchers have tested treatments for women with heavy periods in clinical trials. In these
clinical trials, researchers measure how much blood a woman loses on her period before she has
treatment and after she has treatment to see if there is a difference in the amount of blood she
loses. Researchers can measure blood loss in a laboratory, or women can record how their used
pads or tampons l

.

(secondary supervisor), Mr David Fisher (secondary supervisor) and Dr Natalie Cooper (secondary

milestones, helping her to manage timelines, and to upgrade and complete on time.
Importantly, Claire will support Lily to make decisions regarding the direction of her
research and manage potentially conflicting priorities. She will lead the supervisory panel.
Claire and Lily will meet weekly initially, moving to fortnightly as Lily progresses through
her PhD. Claire will help Lily to set up a Thesis Committee, whose role is to provide degree-
spanning, independent support and advice about academic and training progress.
Claire has supervised 2 PhD and 2 MSc students. She was appointed as Departmental
Graduate Tutor (Research) for the UCL Institute of Clinical Trials and Methodology in 2018,
overseeing the Institute’s post-graduate research (PGR) programme and contributing to
Faculty PGR oversight. She is an active member of the MRC-NIHR Trials and Methodology
Research Partnership Doctoral Training Partnership (TMRP DTP) Leadership Team,
supporting doctoral students across the breadth of clinical trials methodology.

dings from using

clinical trials and systematic reviews, and statistical approaches like regression analyses. I also
gained valuable experience designing and conducting analyses in my dissertation where I
investigated sex differences in the relationship between blood pressure and depression over the
life course, using data from the 1946 British Birth Cohort. I have continued to collaborate on this

departmental meeting. Completion of this degree, and particularly the dissertation, further

implementation, writing ethics applications, and recruiting trial sites. In addition, I learned

resolving unexpected circumstances that can arise during a study.

inings to develop as a leader, attending conferences and conducting research

During undergraduate courses in reproductive physiology, I was struck by the paucity of evidence
in areas of women’s health. I sought work on a cross-sectional study investigating the relationship
between hypertensive disorders of pregnancy and postpartum depression. Under Dr Natalie Dayan
(Research Institute, McGill University Health Centre), I gained first-hand experience extracting
clinical data from medical records into RedCap for analysis. Importantly, this project introduced
me to women’s health research and furthered my interests in this area. I have remained involved in
this study as a manuscript co-author.
Prior to my Masters, I worked with Dr Samira Mubareka (Sunnybrook Research Institute) as a part of
the Royal Society of Canada’s One Health Working Group. I participated in group meetings and
helped to develop and write a policy brief about a One Health approach to zoonoses (REF). This
advanced my writing skills, and I gained experience collaborating with a large multi-disciplinary
team, including a variety of experts, as well as representatives from government and Canada’s
Indigenous communities. This work taught me the importance of and h

eding to inform the design of a mixed-methods survey to understand the impact of
heavy periods on quality of life and women’s experiences seeking treatment. I subsequently
supervised MSc students to seek ethical approval, develop and analyse this survey, a manuscript
for which is in preparation. I have also supervised students to undertake risk of bias assessments
and have overseen a visiting student to assess trustworthiness of the trials. I have worked with the
lead statistician to develop an analysis plan and establish meta-analyses of the primary outcomes.
Results from these meta-analyses are anticipated in May 2025. Complexities that have arisen in
this process have been a key motivation for developing my Fellowship proposal.

patient advocates and women’s health charity representatives (e.g. Wellbeing of Women,

and sought input from attendees through a live survey and discussion regarding which outcomes
should be prioritised.

loses. Researchers can measure blood loss in a laboratory, or women can record how their used
pads or tampons l

ook using picture charts, or answer questionnaires.
Researchers bring together information from all these trials in research called a meta-analysis,
which is a good way to understand which treatments work best. But because blood loss has been
measured in different ways in each clinical trial, it is hard to do a meta-analysis. That means it has
been hard to find out if some treatments work better than others.
Aim and Objectives
In the first part of my research, I will figure out how to best bring together the results from clinical
trials to get better evidence about which treatments reduce bleeding the most. Then I will do
research to understand what doctors, researchers and women think about how blood loss is
measured in clinical trials. This will help researchers do better clinical trials in the future.
Research Methods
I will use a mixture of different research methods during this project. For the first part of my project,
I will carry out a thorough review of all the clinical trials to find all the ways blood loss has been
measured. I will then try to use different statistical methods to find better ways to combine results
from clinical trials that have tested treatments for hea

vy periods.
For the second part I will speak to women, and groups of doctors and researchers to find out their
opinions on ways of measuring blood loss in clinical trials.
Patient and Public Involvement (PPI)

I have spoken to four women who have lived with heavy periods about my research plans. They
agree it is important to do this research so that women with heavy periods get the right treatment.
They noted that it would be important to speak to women of different ages, races, or cultural
backgrounds to understand different opinions on being in clinical trials that measure blood loss.
Therefore, I have planned how to include a diverse group of women in my research interviews.

Review of the existing evidence – how does the existing literature support this
proposal?
What is known about heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) and how does it impact on those
affected?
Approximately one in four women of reproductive age experience HMB, which accounts for
approximately 20% of gynaecology referrals in the NHS (REFs). It is also likely the prevalence of
HMB has been underestimated due to normalisation of symptoms and social stigma surrounding
menstruation. There are many possible causes of HMB which can be classified using the FIGO
classification system 2 (PALM-COEIN) (REF). The FIGO classification system categorises causes
into either structural causes (Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy and hyperplasia) or
non-structural causes (Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet
classified) (REF). However, the mechanisms behind which many of these causes lead to HMB is
still not understood.
HMB is a symptom with life altering implications for women who suffer from it. For example, HMB is
associated with poorer self-rated physical health

In research settings and previous clinical guidelines, HMB has been defined as menstrual blood
loss (MBL) >80ml or a duration of >7days. However, more recently, the National Institute for Health
and Care Excellence (NICE) has changed guidelines to define HMB as excessive menstrual blood
loss which interferes with a woman’s physical, social, emotional and/or material quality of life
(REF). Previous studies suggest that many women only seek treatment for HMB when they fail to
manage symptoms themselves due to the feeling that their symptoms will be dismissed by medical
professionals as ‘trivial’ (Santer 2008). There are also disparities in prevalence of HMB in different
populations. For example, many women begin to experience HMB in perimenopause (REF), and
certain conditions which cause HMB, such as uterine fibroids, are more common in Black women
(REF).
What are the limitations regarding evidence for HMB treatments?
There are many different treatment options available to women suffering from HMB. These include
medical and hormonal treatments, such as contraceptives or tranexamic acid, as well as
procedures or surgeries, such as endometrial ablation or hysterectomy. Since many treat

have evaluated treatments for HMB, there is significant variation in which outcomes have
been reported and how they have been measured (Cooper 2022). As a result of this, a Core
Outcome Set (COS) was developed which identified nine outcomes which should be reported in
future HMB trials (Cooper 2023). While the COS provides a standardised list of outcomes including
menstrual blood loss (MBL), flooding, menstrual cycle metrics, dysmenorrhea, condition-specific
and generic quality of life, adverse events, satisfaction, treatment failure and haemoglobin for
trials, it does not indicate how each of these outcomes should be measured.
MBL is the most reported outcome in HMB trials, however, MBL has been assessed and reported in
many ways (Cooper 2022, Herman 2016). These include quantitative measures of MBL such as the
alkaline-hematin method which is a lab-based method of measuring the blood volume in women’s
menstrual products (REF). Many trials also use versions of pictorial blood loss assessment charts
which have been developed (REFs). These pictorial charts are used by women to count the number

of menstrual products they use and how soiled these products are. However, many of these have
not been validated with modern menstrual products (Magnay 2020). In addition to the quantitative
measures used in trials, there are also many subjective measures of MBL such as having women
rate whether their bleeding is better or worse than before treatment.
Each method has its own associated limitations, both in terms of acceptability to women, and in
consistency or accuracy of the measurement. The different MBL measurement tools have been
previously reviewed, (Magnay 2018), however, this review did not critically assess each tool using
the strict methodology that exists for reviewing measurement tools (COSMIN), and there have been
several studies published which aim to develop or validate MBL measures since the previous
review from 2018.
The array of measurement tools is further compounded by trials making assessments of blood loss
outcomes at a variety of timepoints and using different statistical summaries to report their
findings.
Therefore, there is an urgent need for work in this area to develop guidance on how outcomes
should be measured. Otherwise, the problem of inconsistent out

Variations in how and when MBL is measured, and how it is reported, present complex challenges
for evidence synthesis. Subsets of trials with consistent handling of MBL data may be analysed
whilst other evidence is excluded, with clear implications for risk-of-bias and strength of evidence.
Attempts to synthesise more comprehensively may result in confused or biased interpretations
due to data variations not being handled appropriately. This work package will explore different
statistical approaches that may help to meta-analyse data more appropriately and completely
from trials where a measure of MBL is reported.
MBL Reported at Different Timepoints
Post-treatment measurement of MBL may take place at various lengths of time after
randomisation, typically between 3 and 12 months. To address this, I will investigate the strength of
correlation between MBL measures collected at different timepoints (for example, 3 and 6
months), and thereby assess whether it is more appropriate to analyse such data separately or
together. For example, do MBL measures collected at 3 months

ich measures of menstrual blood loss are acceptable
and prioritised by women and trial investigators.

determine which MBL measure should be used in clinical trials.
In this work package, I will conduct focus groups and interviews with women with lived experience
of HMB and HMB clinical trial investigators and staff to understand which measures are acceptable
to different stakeholders. I will use a mixture of online and in person discussions to better develop

my skills in conducting interviews and focus groups. The topic guides for the interviews and focus
groups will be developed based on findings from my formal evaluations of different MBL measures
in work package 1.

Due to the potentially personal nature of discussing HMB and MBL, I have chosen to conduct
interviews with women with lived experience of HMB instead of focus groups. I will aim to recruit

inform consistent MBL measurement in future HMB trials.
Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and/or
wellbeing of the public and/or to patients/service users, carers, and health
and care services?
An estimated one in four women of reproductive age are affected by heavy menstrual bleeding
(HMB)(REF). Women with HMB suffer lower quality of life due to both physical symptoms as well as
restrictions on their social and work lives. Women with menstrual health conditions make up a key
proportion of the 755,000 women waiting for gynaecology services in the UK, with many women
waiting over a year for treatment (REF). These delays in diagnosis and treatment leave women
suffering worsening symptoms and poor quality of life for extended periods of time. This can also
lead to more complex treatments being needed when they are eventually seen by gynaecology
services. Therefore, it is crucial that HMB research is prioritised now.
While there are many medical and surgical treatment options available to treat those who
experience HMB, high-quality evidence synthesis to guide policy and practice i

s limited. The
women we have spoken to have stressed the need for better quality evidence as currently many
women try various treatments before finding one which is effective. The evidence of treatment
effectiveness has been hindered in a large part by inconsistency of outcome measurements used
across relevant clinical trials. For example, menstrual blood loss (MBL) and quality of life (QoL)
have been evaluated using a range of instruments, and at a variety of time-points, which
complicates meta-analysis and reduces reliability of findings.

Therefore, this research is vitally important to people affected by HMB. The proposed research will
provide more reliable evidence about which treatments are most effective in reducing MBL and
reduce research waste from trials that currently are not contributing to evidence syntheses. It will
also explore whether reduced MBL leads to improvement in other outcomes that are important to
women, including QoL. This is based on feedback from PPI groups that reduced MBL is only
important if it also results in improved QoL. Lastly, this project will provide foundations for the
development of a measure or tool that can be used to reliably measure MBL in clinical trials, taking
the needs of women (including women from different age-groups, regions and ethnicities), trialists,
and other stakeholders (e.g. guideline developers, regulatory authorities) into account. The current
measures of MBL used in clinical trials can be burdensome, and it is unclear whether these
measures are acceptable to all women. Therefore, this research might greatly improve women’s
participation in future trials, and the applicability of future trials and evidence syntheses.
Ultimately, this will facilitate efficient

h variations in outcome reporting in future meta-
analyses of HMB trials, or in other clinical areas.
Informing Guideline Developers and Regulatory Processes to Improve Health
As part of my training plan, I will do a work placement with the MHRA which will be organised with
the help of my collaborator Dr Jahnavi Daru. During this work placement, I will have the opportunity
to present my findings and explore how my findings might facilitate more efficient decision making
within the agency around licensing of treatments.
I will also use my team’s current collaborations with NICE to explore how my research findings may
be incorporated into future NICE guidelines for treatment of HMB.
The Impact on Women and the NHS
Apart from the significant impacts of my findings on future research in HMB, my work to better
estimate the effects of treatments on MBL and QoL will inform guidelines and medical practice. By
making use of all the available trials and reducing research waste, my findings will help to
efficiently provide women with effective treatment options for HMB. This will lead to significantly
improved quality of life for women with HMB and will reduce the burden of HMB within the NHS

Please describe your research career to date (1000 words)
Previous Experience:
I am an early-career researcher with a strong interest in improving evidence synthesis in women’s
health. I have a BSc in Physiology (McGill University), an MSc in Population Health (UCL), and
experience working on a variety of health research projects.
As an undergraduate, I spent two summers as a research assistant with Dr Rosemary Martino,
University of Toronto. Initially, I evaluated abstracts for inclusion in a systematic review assessing
the benefits of dysphagia screening in stroke patients. This introduced me to systematic reviews
and gave me insight into their importance in healthcare research. I subsequently helped develop an
online mixed-methods survey of speech-language pathologist practices in paediatric dysphagia;
developing skills in SPSS by tabulating survey response frequencies, means

further solidified my interest in women’s health research and showed me the huge impact research
can have on the lives of women. It gave me greater insight and experience in clinical trial design and
implementation, writing ethics applications, and recruiting trial sites. In addition, I learned
important transferrable skills through managing multiple tasks simultaneously and effectively
resolving unexpected circumstances that can arise during a study.
Current Employment:
In November 2023, I started my current position (Research Assistant, Meta-Analysis Group, MRC
Clinical Trials Unit at UCL), funded by a National Institute of Health Research Health Technology
Assessment Award (NIHR153187), to synthesise results from clinical trials which have assessed
treatments for heavy menstrual bleeding. This role has combined my research interests in
women’s health and evidence synthesis.
My duties have included developing protocols, conducting literature searches, identifying eligible
clinical trials, developing data extraction forms, and completing data extraction across 120 eligible
trials. Importantly, I helped to host a workshop involving women with lived experience of heavy
menstrual ble

eding to inform the design of a mixed-methods survey to understand the impact of
heavy periods on quality of life and women’s experiences seeking treatment. I subsequently
supervised MSc students to seek ethical approval, develop and analyse this survey, a manuscript
for which is in preparation. I have also supervised students to undertake risk of bias assessments
and have overseen a visiting student to assess trustworthiness of the trials. I have worked with the
lead statistician to develop an analysis plan and establish meta-analyses of the primary outcomes.
Results from these meta-analyses are anticipated in May 2025. Complexities that have arisen in
this process have been a key motivation for developing my Fellowship proposal.
In early 2024, I was central in organising a Menstrual Health Research Day, to inform women,
patient advocates and women’s health charity representatives (e.g. Wellbeing of Women,
Menstrual Health Project) about our research. During the event, I presented our research plans
and sought input from attendees through a live survey and discussion regarding which outcomes
should be prioritised.
I was also invited to co-author a WHO-commissioned Cochrane review

comparing intravenous
versus oral iron in treating iron-deficiency anaemia in pregnancy (REF). As first author, I identified
and evaluated relevant clinical trials, collected and meta-analysed data, completed risk of bias,
trustworthiness and GRADE assessments for each outcome included in the review, as well as
contributed to writing the manuscript. From this, I gained extensive experience in every stage of
conducting systematic reviews and meta-analyses and expanded my network among other

Plain English Summary of Research (600 words)
Background Information
One in four women will have heavy periods, which means they lose a lot of blood during their period
or have a period that lasts a long time. They might also feel tired, have pain and need to miss days
of school or work. These things can all make women’s lives harder.
Researchers have tested treatments for women with heavy periods in clinical trials. In these
clinical trials, researchers measure how much blood a woman loses on her period before she has
treatment and after she has treatment to see if there is a difference in the amount of blood she
loses. Researchers can measure blood loss in a laboratory, or women can record how their used
pads or tampons l

ook using picture charts, or answer questionnaires.
Researchers bring together information from all these trials in research called a meta-analysis,
which is a good way to understand which treatments work best. But because blood loss has been
measured in different ways in each clinical trial, it is hard to do a meta-analysis. That means it has
been hard to find out if some treatments work better than others.
Aim and Objectives
In the first part of my research, I will figure out how to best bring together the results from clinical
trials to get better evidence about which treatments reduce bleeding the most. Then I will do
research to understand what doctors, researchers and women think about how blood loss is
measured in clinical trials. This will help researchers do better clinical trials in the future.
Research Methods
I will use a mixture of different research methods during this project. For the first part of my project,
I will carry out a thorough review of all the clinical trials to find all the ways blood loss has been
measured. I will then try to use different statistical methods to find better ways to combine results
from clinical trials that have tested treatments for hea

vy periods.
For the second part I will speak to women, and groups of doctors and researchers to find out their
opinions on ways of measuring blood loss in clinical trials.

I have spoken to four women who have lived with heavy periods about my research plans. They
agree it is important to do this research so that women with heavy periods get the right treatment.
They noted that it would be important to speak to women of different ages, races, or cultural
backgrounds to understand different opinions on being in clinical trials that measure blood loss.
Therefore, I have planned how to include a diverse group of women in my research interviews.

what questions I should ask. They will also help me to present my findings so that members of the

Review of the existing evidence – how does the existing literature support this
proposal?
What is known about heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) and how does it impact on those
affected?
Approximately one in four women of reproductive age experience HMB, which accounts for
approximately 20% of gynaecology referrals in the NHS (REFs). It is also likely the prevalence of
HMB has been underestimated due to normalisation of symptoms and social stigma surrounding
menstruation. There are many possible causes of HMB which can be classified using the FIGO
classification system 2 (PALM-COEIN) (REF). The FIGO classification system categorises causes
into either structural causes (Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy and hyperplasia) or
non-structural causes (Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet
classified) (REF). However, the mechanisms behind which many of these causes lead to HMB is
still not understood.
HMB is a symptom with life altering implications for women who suffer from it. For example, HMB is
associated with poorer self-rated physical health

In research settings and previous clinical guidelines, HMB has been defined as menstrual blood
loss (MBL) >80ml or a duration of >7days. However, more recently, the National Institute for Health
and Care Excellence (NICE) has changed guidelines to define HMB as excessive menstrual blood
loss which interferes with a woman’s physical, social, emotional and/or material quality of life
(REF). Previous studies suggest that many women only seek treatment for HMB when they fail to
manage symptoms themselves due to the feeling that their symptoms will be dismissed by medical
professionals as ‘trivial’ (Santer 2008). There are also disparities in prevalence of HMB in different
populations. For example, many women begin to experience HMB in perimenopause (REF), and
certain conditions which cause HMB, such as uterine fibroids, are more common in Black women
(REF).
What are the limitations regarding evidence for HMB treatments?
There are many different treatment options available to women suffering from HMB. These include
medical and hormonal treatments, such as contraceptives or tranexamic acid, as well as
procedures or surgeries, such as endometrial ablation or hysterectomy. Since many treat

have evaluated treatments for HMB, there is significant variation in which outcomes have
been reported and how they have been measured (Cooper 2022). As a result of this, a Core
Outcome Set (COS) was developed which identified nine outcomes which should be reported in
future HMB trials (Cooper 2023). While the COS provides a standardised list of outcomes including
menstrual blood loss (MBL), flooding, menstrual cycle metrics, dysmenorrhea, condition-specific
and generic quality of life, adverse events, satisfaction, treatment failure and haemoglobin for
trials, it does not indicate how each of these outcomes should be measured.
MBL is the most reported outcome in HMB trials, however, MBL has been assessed and reported in
many ways (Cooper 2022, Herman 2016). These include quantitative measures of MBL such as the
alkaline-hematin method which is a lab-based method of measuring the blood volume in women’s
menstrual products (REF). Many trials also use versions of pictorial blood loss assessment charts
which have been developed (REFs). These pictorial charts are used by women to count the number

of menstrual products they use and how soiled these products are. However, many of these have
not been validated with modern menstrual products (Magnay 2020). In addition to the quantitative
measures used in trials, there are also many subjective measures of MBL such as having women
rate whether their bleeding is better or worse than before treatment.
Each method has its own associated limitations, both in terms of acceptability to women, and in
consistency or accuracy of the measurement. The different MBL measurement tools have been
previously reviewed, (Magnay 2018), however, this review did not critically assess each tool using
the strict methodology that exists for reviewing measurement tools (COSMIN), and there have been
several studies published which aim to develop or validate MBL measures since the previous
review from 2018.
The array of measurement tools is further compounded by trials making assessments of blood loss
outcomes at a variety of timepoints and using different statistical summaries to report their
findings.
Therefore, there is an urgent need for work in this area to develop guidance on how outcomes
should be measured. Otherwise, the problem of inconsistent out

Research Question
How can we improve meta-analyses of MBL outcomes in clinical trials comparing treatment
options for HMB?
Objectives
1. To critically assess the measurement tools used to assess MBL in clinical trials.
2. To explore statistical approaches to improve meta-analysis for the different MBL outcomes
reported.
3. To determine what is acceptable and prioritised to women and trialists when measuring
MBL in HMB trials.
Methods and Timelines for Delivery

different measurement tools used to assess MBL in clinical trials and critically evaluating these
tools.
Work Package 2 (Months 9-18) - Exploring statistical approaches for meta-analyses and applying
these so that more clinical trial data is included in meta-analyses of the MBL outcome.
Work Package 3 (Months 19-30) - Conducting interviews and focus groups with women and trial
teams to determine which aspects of MBL measures are acceptable and prioritised by different
stakeholders.
